speaker: interviewer
text: Zisblatt, tape seven. Irene, we were talking about the night that you and Sabka were rescued by the General Patton's Third Division.

speaker: interviewee
text: Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Third Army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in the Red Cross trucks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were in the Red Cross truck. And that night, we-- we didn't sleep much. We just talked about things we weren't able to talk about before. And I-- Sabka was in the camp like almost six years before we got liberated. So she knew more. She experienced more. She saw more than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were a lot of things. Like the nurse in the hospital that helped us survive. I said to her, Sabka can you figure out why all these people were chosen to have all these different experiments? And the two of us are here, and we never saw the others? Why? How did we come-- how did we do it? Why did we do it? And she said, you know the nurse in the hospital that was working with the-- with the Germans and the doctor? Well, she was underground. And because of her, we got out of that experiment.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other experiment, she said, we were just strong enough to do it. The others probably weren't strong enough to do it. But this was a fatal-- this was like the last experiment that we were supposed to die. And she--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the one that they experimented on taking our number out of the arm. And she-- the nurse, she explained to me, the nurse saved our lives because she was underground. And she was trying to save as many lives as she could. And if not for her, we would be dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, but how can she do it? She-- they didn't kill her for doing that? And she said, that well, they didn't catch her. They didn't see what she was doing. She-- she gave us new numbers and new names so they'd never find out. So that's why we have these numbers. And these numbers. And we were supposed to forget about our original numbers for that reason. So she explained a lot of stuff. How did-- how did Sabka know this?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was talking to the nurse more because like she knew what to ask in the short time that she had the opportunity to ask. But I didn't because I was so bewildered and so new to this. And-- and I just-- I just-- I was afraid more than she was, probably. And she just had more-- more knowledge of what was happening to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Sabka mention the possibility that there were other people working with the nurse? That maybe there was some kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't, but I'm sure there were. I'm sure-- if they weren't in the hospital, they were somewheres outside. In the-- in the crematorium maybe, the sonderkommando. Maybe the partisans on the outside of the camps. Because there was a lot of talk that there is-- you know, the sonderkommando has access to outside information. And when they blew up the crematorium in Birkenau, that was with the help of the partisans from the outside. So it was-- she could have been part of that. But we were lucky we had her. That she helped us to survive and maybe others before that that we weren't aware.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they take you that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: That night, we stayed in the woods. We didn't-- they-- they did whatever they had to do. And then-- and then in the morning, they wanted to know whether there is-- where we lived. They thought maybe we lived nearby somewheres. And we said that, you know, we lived in different countries. But we would like to go to a hospital maybe. Or-- we didn't know where. Somewhere where it's safe. So they decided that the hospital would be the best place to take us. Because they had a war to fight. It was still January '94. April '94. '95 actually.

speaker: interviewee
text: '45. Oh my god. In '45. And actually, the white flags were out. But there was a lot of fighting going on still everywhere. And then we found out the war with Japan was still on. So they were-- they had their own responsibilities.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took-- well, that night when finally we went to sleep and wake up in the morning, my friend Sabka was dead. She was dead in the morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was dead in the morning. She died in-- in her sleep. And-- and we didn't know why she died, because she was OK the night before. But then the doctor from the army, from the-- from the army, he said that she probably had typhoid. And because it's going-- you know, that's very common of-- in the war. And because they said, well, maybe we'd ought to take her to the hospital and they take an autopsy. And I said, no way. No. She-- she didn't want to become ashes. I'm sure she didn't want to be cut up either. So we buried her in the woods. And there, I went back the route that we came up, and I found her white blanket that she was so fond of. And we wrapped her up in the blanket, and we buried her there. And they assured me that they will take her. When-- when they can, they will come back and they will take her in to a cemetery. But that never happened because they-- they never-- of course, I was in the hospital after that. And I was in-- I was in and out of incoherent and God knows what. And then after we-- I was well, we did go back into the woods to look for her. But there was no mark. We just left a little marker. But we couldn't find exactly where-- where she was. So she is just there. But she is not ashes and that makes me feel good, that she did not become ashes. Because that was her only thing. Let's not become ashes. Don't let them make me be ashes. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you in hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in the Pilsen area. It was an army hospital. It was an American army hospital. Before that it was a German army hospital. Because there were still like German soldiers that were wounded that was there. But they-- they blocked them off into a different section and-- when-- when they got. See they brought me in there, and then they went back to trace the route, and they found other people that needed hospital care. So they brought them in. A lot of them didn't make it out of the hospital. But then from the hospital, in the end of that summer-- we were-- the army-- we were under the army's care this whole time. And they were being sent back. The war was finally over. And they were being sent back home. And then they took us to Salzburg, Austria into a DP camp. And this is-- this was close to 1946. And I was in the DP camp with, again, with people from Poland, from different countries. Waiting for borders to open up, waiting-- waiting for papers to come through to go to different countries. Most of the people were hoping to go to Palestine. But that was closed, too. And so whoever made it to Palestine had to be like smuggled into Palestine. And we tried that also at one time. But we did get to Italy. And we laid on the beaches for five days and five nights waiting for a ship to come and get us. But there was no ship. And-- and we were very still weak, recuperating from the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you go with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just kids that-- well, there were a couple of leaders that were very devoted, Haganah people and Zionists. And they had to do-- you know, they came in one night. And they said, whoever wants to get-- you know, go to Palestine, take a chance having hard times until we get there. Because we might be caught, and sent back, and all that. So we took a chance. A truckload full. And we went to Italy. Most of the people, we didn't even know each other. We were just Jews from different places and survivors of the concentration camp looking for a-- for a home. And a lot of us had to come back because we got sick. And I was one of them. And I came back to the DP camp and stayed there till 1947. People that went back home, like soldiers from the army, they took my background and information. And they put my name and everything into the papers in New York, to the Jewish paper, and to all the other papers. And my father's brother lived in the Bronx. And he read my-- my article in the Jewish-- in the And he got in touch with me immediately. And I waited until 1947 until he was able to sponsor me out. And on November 8, under Irene [? Fletcher, ?] I arrived to New York and met my family that I never knew before.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you never thought to go home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Back to Polana? After the war, right away I wanted to go home, just in case an uncle, or an aunt, or a cousin would come back and be alive. But I was a child, and I was under the children's quota. And the people that were responsible for me in the DP camp, they said, we can only be responsible for you until the border. And once you get across the border, you're on your own. And the Russians are over there. And it's not very pretty there. And chances are there is nobody there. So we will help you better get in touch with somebody that's there, and then they can tell you if anybody came home. And that's what I did. I got in touch with the owner of the resort, my father's friend. But one of their sons. And I got and answer. And they wrote me that nobody is home. And there is no sense for me to come home. So I never went home. But I would like to go one day when my children are able to go. I would like to show them where their roots are. And to go for [INAUDIBLE], because my grandparents and my brother are buried there. So-- but I have it taken care of. People that live there are taking care of, the cemeteries, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came to the United States, what did you do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school at night, and I went to work. I worked. I-- I-- while I was going to school to learn the language, I worked in a bakery during the day. And then when I finished-- I-- when I school-- and I-- I only really took high school equivalency in English. And then I went to work for RCA. And I was working in the-- in the engineer department, an assistant to making to-- the inside of the tube. And then I met my husband, and I got married, and I started to raise a family.

words.text: in a bakery during the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was an American born.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live after you were married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we lived in New Jersey. I lived there before I was married, too. And I was very active in B'nai Brith. And I was president of BBYO at the time. And-- and I was-- we had an Israeli bond drive in Beth El Temple in South Orange. And I was chairman of the drive. And-- and I was selling the bonds. I met my husband. He was one of the buyers. And we went together for eight months and got married. We got married the same year we met. And we were very happy together. We had two children. And at the age of 40, he-- he died of a heart attack. So I have many losses in my life. [CRYING] Thank God I have the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Irene, how-- how did this experience-- you came from an Orthodox family. How does this experience shape your beliefs--

speaker: interviewee
text: Today.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in God today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm getting better at it. When I-- when I was in the camp, of course, I didn't-- I didn't believe that God was there for me. And I couldn't understand if he was there, why did he just do nothing about what was happening? Why did he let it happen?

speaker: interviewer
text: And I still have that fight with him. I still feel that he let it happen. And maybe some of the people he couldn't save. But why didn't he save the children? They were-- they were so little, they were so innocent, and they didn't do anything wrong in their lives. Why did they die? And I just still have that fight with him. And I just can't.

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel I don't owe him the Orthodox upbringing that I was taught. Because I don't feel comfortable with it. I don't understand why why all this-- this has happened without any reason. And I don't understand why it is so important for me to be that Orthodox Jew that I was expected to be. I feel that it's OK what I am. I'm comfortable with what I am. And I believe that my heritage-- my heritage is very important to me. And I never denied that I was a Jew. And I was always a good Jew. And I intend to be for the rest of my life. And I-- and I feel that I have this fight with him that I-- I don't owe him that. And this is what I can do at this time. Maybe tomorrow I will be better. Maybe next year I will be better. I'll be more into the Orthodox. But as of now, I can't. The only thing I do in Orthodox is I go on Yom Kippor to the Orthodox temple. And I say Yizkor for my family that perished in Auschwitz in the Orthodox temple. Because I don't think they would understand if I would say it in any other language.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would your message be to Jews today and younger Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: The young Jews today should be very aware of what happened and learn from the atrocities that happened to Jewish culture and to Jewish life at that time. And to protect the Jewish life so it never happens again to any of our children, or grandchildren, or the generations to come. And everybody has a way of doing things. And I-- and I know that everybody can do a little bit of something to make it better. To heal the hate, to heal the-- the-- the killings, and-- and all that is going on int the world. Because I-- I don't see that anybody had learned from what happened in the past. And the history-- Jewish or not Jewish-- it has to be changed. People have to care more, people have to do more about protecting the future from what had happened in the past.

speaker: interviewer
text: Irene, thank you very much for sharing with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. [? But ?] [? Bill ?] send me a copy of this so you'll have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: It probably in the [INAUDIBLE], too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh yeah. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- this is my father's oldest brother and his family. That's-- this is his wife and his three children. And I remember this picture in my grandparents' house and-- before the war. That's why this picture is like so important. It's very, very close to me. And they live-- they came-- my-- my uncle, my father's brother, he came out here before the First World War. Because when I came here, he was already here 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. These-- these are my father's two sisters. He only had two sisters. My Aunt Fanny and my Aunt Rose. And in Jewish, [? Figa ?] and [? Rucha. ?] And they came out here shortly after the First World War and they-- they-- they lived here all-- most of their lives in New Jersey.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Just wait a second, please. [? CREW: ?] And they weren't Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is another brother of my father's. And he did go through Auschwitz with us. And he survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Friedman Zeigelstein. And his wife is also a survivor. They met after the war. When he is the one that went home to look for things and met her. And they got married. Then they went to Israel and-- because her family was there from before the war. And he died there about three years ago in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken when?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was taken after the war. In-- in Polana. And he sent it to me to the United States before he went to Israel. Yeah. This is a picture that was taken way before the Holocaust. And all these people in it are people that lived in my town and also people that lived in neighboring towns. A few of these people on this-- in this picture survived. Most of them didn't. And I can count how many. 1, 2, 3, 4-- five people on this picture survived in the-- in the-- from the Holocaust. And these were all from my-- my town and neighboring town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any relatives in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: There is one boy, a cousin. And he didn't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And his name is Nuethe Mermelstein. And he was my-- mt mother's uncle's son. My-- my-- my grandmother's on my-- mother's mother's brother's son. As far-- as close as I can figure it out. And the rest of them were people from town that lived in town. A few people are still alive today. Like one, two. And this person is the one that was the sister of my father's cousin that I shared the soup with so she survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is a picture of me after the war in the DP camp. And I was, I think, just about 14 there. But I look like I'm 40 because I'm still trying to get all the stuff out of my system that shouldn't be there. So I was like always like swollen. You know, my body was swollen. I was still under the care of the doctors getting all the right nutrition into the body. Zeigelstein-- My maiden name. My Jewish name. Salzburg. The 19th of--

speaker: interviewer
text: November.

speaker: interviewee
text: June.

speaker: interviewer
text: June.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is six?

speaker: interviewer
text: 36? It looks like a 10 to me. 10, with an X and a 1.

speaker: interviewee
text: X and a 1. The 10th.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is a picture also of me and-- in the DP camp still. But I was already well there. So you can see the difference that my body wasn't so swollen anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Taken when?

speaker: interviewee
text: But this was taken November of '46. About a year after liberation. How about the picture when I was 12 years old?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a picture of me when I was 12 years old just before the Holocaust. And that's the only thing I have left and that-- that I found here in one of my-- my cousin's house that was sent here before the war. And when I look at that background, I want to go in there and pick the plums and the apples off of those trees that are in the back of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Polana in-- in our backyard, the back of the house. This was taken in 1943 just before we were taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exact date. [? Tzeitsel, ?] that lived in Philadelphia at the time, came to visit my grandparents, which was my grandfather's sister. And she took pictures of everybody to take back to the United States. And that's when she took this. And there was another picture that she took of my sister. And she was only-- she was crawling. I don't think she was--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the picture of the ghetto in [? Munkacs ?] in [? Schiowitz ?] where we were. This road that you see on this picture, the railroad track used to be there leading in to where these cars are parked. There are cars, right? There-- there used to be the shacks and the huts that we put together from table cloths and blankets that we lived in.

speaker: interviewee
text: This mountain in the background is the prison. And in front of it, between the ghetto and that prison, was all water. And the machine guns were right alongside of this-- these walls and on top guarding the ghetto so nobody can escape. OK. This is a map that shows where the ghetto is and the distance between the ghetto and my home. And then the route to Auschwitz. All this distance. Now I can understand why we were on that train for so long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is the route of your march on here as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: The death march is on another map.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's the map.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is a map of the distance between Auschwitz and the labor camp I was sent to. And it's-- it's over here in the upper Sudeten Deutschland. And it's called-- the town is called Neuengamme at that time. It's Neuengamme. And that's where the labor camp was right here. And then the death march started over here and back tracked through here. And somewheres down here. And see there is Dresden. And all the towns that I mentioned that I came-- that I crossed, I remember seeing. And then somewheres near Pilsen in these woods, I escaped and liberated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know exactly whether it was on this side or this side. But we were taken to Theresienstadt. But we never reached Theresienstadt. It's somewheres in here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wedding picture to my husband and my children's father. And we were married in South Orange, New Jersey on October 27, 1956. And on the right side of us is my father's sister. And she gave me away. And somewheres on this side there in the back of my husband is my father's brother. And the two of them gave me away because my parents were not able to be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband's name was Herman Weisberg. This is a picture of my daughter, and her husband, and her daughter. And her name is Robin June Weisberg, her maiden name. And her husband, Kevin and her daughter Hayley.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my son, Marc, and his wife, Pamela. And they live in New Jersey. And they got married three years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in-- they were engaged. When did they get engaged? February of--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: '92. And they got married shortly after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go on, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my grandson, Skyler. He is Marc's son and he is eight months old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Taken when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken in-- just about a month ago, which is-- it's November-- I guess in September.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture that was taken at my father's cousin's grandchild's bat mitzvah three years ago. And these people in this picture are all survivors. My cousin-- my father's first cousin, which is my second cousin-- she is today 94 years old. And she is a survivor of Auschwitz. And this is her daughter, and her niece, and another niece, and myself. And the rest of the people are-- all the women are survivors from the same town. And the men are from neighboring towns that they married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Introduce them.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter, Robin, and my little granddaughter, Hayley. And she is two years old. And I love them both.

speaker: interviewer
text: Robin, is there anything that you'd--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just that we're really proud of my mom. And just all the work that she's done with the Holocaust, and bringing her story to other people, and doing all the things that she does. And I hope that she feels happy in her family. And that she finally has a family that are all here for her. And we love her.
